items.title: Pre-mortem: prospective hindsight to surface failure modes
items.claim: The pre-mortem framework covers a structured way to imagine that a decision has already failed and then work backward to name the reasons, using prospective hindsight to surface failure modes and assumptions that forward planning tends to hide. It describes a general reasoning technique for a class of decisions, not what may happen in any one person's situation.
items.limitations: This is a framework-level technique drawn from curated decision-science literature, so it structures how risks and assumptions are surfaced rather than measuring outcomes or predicting an individual's result.

items.title: Reversibility: one-way vs two-way door decisions
items.claim: The reversibility framework covers how to classify a choice as a reversible two-way door or a hard-to-undo one-way door, and how the degree of reversibility tends to shape how much deliberation and commitment a decision may warrant. It offers a way to frame a class of decisions rather than a forecast for any individual choice.
items.limitations: This is a framework-level lens from curated decision-science material, so it helps frame commitment and reversibility but does not measure or predict outcomes for a specific person.

items.title: Values and tradeoffs: separating values, alternatives, and tradeoffs
items.claim: This framework covers structured ways to separate what someone values from the alternatives available and the tradeoffs between them, so that competing objectives can be made explicit rather than collapsed into a single score. It describes how to organize reasoning about a class of decisions, not which option may suit any individual.
items.limitations: This is a framework-level approach from curated decision-science literature, so it clarifies values and tradeoffs without measuring outcomes or recommending a choice for any individual.

items.title: Smallest decisive experiment: value-of-information framing
items.claim: This framework covers value-of-information reasoning and the idea of choosing the smallest, cheapest, time-boxed experiment that could meaningfully change a decision, paired with a pre-committed signal for what counts as a pass or fail. It describes a general way to reduce uncertainty for a class of decisions rather than a prediction about any individual's result.
items.limitations: This is a framework-level technique from curated decision-science material, so it guides how to test assumptions cheaply rather than measuring or forecasting outcomes for a specific person.
